sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 17, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 16 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Something strange happened! The player tried to move LEFT but the output shows the player is STILL at cols 20-23 (node 4: W at [[28, 20], [31, 20]], node 5: N at [[28, 21], [28, 23], [31, 23], [31, 21]]). The player didn't move!
This means the player was BLOCKED from moving LEFT. What blocked it? The blue square at cols 17-18 is inside the frame at cols 16-19. But the frame disappeared earlier. The blue center is still there at [[29, 17], [29, 18], [30, 18], [30, 17]].
So the blue center acts as a solid obstacle that blocks the player from moving through it. The player is at cols 20-23, and trying to move LEFT would put the player at cols 16-19, which overlaps with the blue center at cols 17-18. So the move was blocked.
This confirms: blue centers are solid obstacles.
Now I understand the game better:
1. The player is a 4x4 block (green body + white top)
2. Blue centers are 2x2 solid obstacles
3. Dark gray frames are 4x4 hollow squares that disappear when the player overlaps them and reappear when the player moves away
4. The horizontal bar is a 4x12 obstacle that splits when the player moves through it and regenerates when the player moves away
5. The player cannot move through blue centers
So what's the goal? The player can't reach the left blue square because it's blocked by the blue center itself. Similarly, the center blue square is below the bar, and the right blue square is above and to the right.
Wait, maybe the goal isn't to collect the blue squares. Maybe the goal is something else entirely.
Let me reconsider. The efficiency budget says ~71 target actions. That's a lot. Maybe the game involves navigating around the obstacles to reach a specific exit point, or maybe it involves some kind of pattern matching.
Actually, let me look at the bottom-right corner more carefully. Node 12 (c, pixels=5, boundary=[[63, 59], [63, 63]]). This is a dark gray strip at the bottom. It was pixels=3 earlier, then pixels=4, now pixels=5. It's growing! This might be a counter or progress bar.
Wait, but the instructions say to ignore HUD elements. Let me check if this is gameplay or HUD.
Actually, looking at the image, there's a pink bar at the bottom (row 63). The dark gray strip at [[63, 59], [63, 63]] is on the same row. This might be part of the HUD.
Let me reconsider the game. Maybe the goal is to navigate the player to a specific position, like the top-right corner or somewhere else. Or maybe the goal is to visit all three blue square positions without touching the blue centers.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the initial state. The player started at rows 40-43, cols 40-43 (below and to the right of the center blue square). The player moved UP and LEFT to reach the bar's row. Maybe the goal is to navigate to all three blue square positions, going around the obstacles.
Let me think about this differently. The three blue squares are at:
1. Left: rows 28-31, cols 16-19 (frame), blue center at rows 29-30, cols 17-18
2. Center: rows 36-39, cols 32-35 (frame), blue center at rows 37-38, cols 33-34
3. Right: rows 24-27, cols 44-47 (frame), blue center at rows 25-26, cols 45-46
The player is currently at rows 28-31, cols 20-23. The player can't reach the left blue square because it's blocked. The center blue square is below at rows 36-39. The right blue square is above and to the right at rows 24-27, cols 44-47.
Maybe the goal is to reach the right blue square? Let me try moving RIGHT and UP to get there.
Actually, let me first go back to the starting position and think more carefully. I've wasted 16 actions already. Let me try to figure out the goal by exploring more systematically.
Let me try moving DOWN to go below the bar, then RIGHT to go toward the right blue square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player blocked from moving LEFT (blue center obstacle)
# Player at rows 28-31, cols 20-23
# Let me try moving RIGHT to go back toward the bar, then navigate to the right blue square

# First, let me check if I can move RIGHT
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After RIGHT:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
print("Gameplay changed:", result.get('gameplay_changed'))
print("No impact:", result.get('no_impact'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
